\title{Source-Heldout Evaluation of Physical, Neural, and Frozen-Encoder Signals for AI-Generated Image Detection}
\author{Anonymous Author(s)}
\begin{document}
\maketitle

\begin{abstract}
AI-generated image detection is commonly reported as closed-set accuracy, yet forensic deployment requires stable ranking, calibration, and operating points under generator shift. We evaluate this gap with a repeated-seed benchmark spanning conventional physical/signal features, ResNet-18, physics-guided feature fusion, frozen foundation encoders, and score-level SCP-Fusion. On Ishu same-domain splits, 'combined\_v3' and ResNet-18 tie at 0.8246 and 0.8246 accuracy, while physics-guided fusion improves to 0.8450 accuracy and 0.9177 AUC. Under Ishu to source-balanced MS COCOAI transfer, frozen CLIP is the strongest standalone ranker at 0.8641 AUC, whereas fusion variants expose calibration and fake-call-rate shifts. In the reverse direction, source-capped tuned fusion reaches 0.7632 accuracy and 0.8361 AUC with a 0.5175 target fake-call rate. These results support reporting AI-image forensics as a source-heldout evaluation problem with separate ranking, calibration, thresholding, and triage evidence.
\end{abstract}

\section{Claim-Evidence Checklist}
This generated checklist mirrors the repository claim-evidence matrix so paper claims stay tied to concrete artifacts and caveats during editing.

\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{source\_shift\_splits\_metrics}: Same-domain detector performance is not enough for AI-image forensics; ranking, calibration, and default decisions diverge under source shift. \textit{Evidence artifact:} reports/assets/publication\_core\_results.md. \textit{Caveat:} Do not frame this as state of the art; frame it as a source-heldout diagnostic benchmark.
\item \textbf{physics\_guided\_branch\_helps}: Physics-guided ResNet fusion improves over vanilla ResNet and combined\_v3 on Ishu same-domain and gives a useful reverse-transfer calibration anchor. \textit{Evidence artifact:} reports/physics\_guided\_vs\_resnet\_2026\_06\_12.md. \textit{Caveat:} This is a single-image physical/signal proxy, not true multi-light photometric stereo; vanilla ResNet still wins some in-domain MS COCOAI checks.
\item \textbf{clip\_transfer\_frontier}: Frozen CLIP ViT-B/32 is the strongest standalone transfer-ranking and high-confidence triage branch in the current benchmark. \textit{Evidence artifact:} reports/assets/publication\_score\_fusion\_clip\_frontier.png. \textit{Caveat:} CLIP has strong ranking but reverse-transfer default thresholds still over-call generated images.
\item \textbf{scp\_fusion\_is\_diagnostic}: SCP-Fusion is currently most defensible as a diagnostic fusion protocol: it combines physical, neural, and foundation signals, but score-level fusion does not always beat the best standalone branch. \textit{Evidence artifact:} reports/assets/publication\_reverse\_fusion\_tradeoff.png. \textit{Caveat:} Current score fusion can suppress the best branch and can inherit source-threshold bias.
\item \textbf{source\_thresholding\_improves\_decisions}: Source-aware operating-point selection can turn strong but biased reverse-transfer ranking into better binary decisions. \textit{Evidence artifact:} reports/assets/publication\_reverse\_operating\_points.png. \textit{Caveat:} The capped source-threshold result is an operating point, not a learned general solution.
\item \textbf{combined\_v4\_is\_ablation\_candidate}: combined\_v4 is useful as a richer physical/signal ablation, especially with feature selection, but the completed transfer gate does not justify replacing combined\_v3 as the main conventional baseline. \textit{Evidence artifact:} reports/combined\_v4\_full\_transfer\_summary\_2026\_06\_13.md. \textit{Caveat:} Raw v4 mainly helps transfer accuracy, while select-k60 helps transfer AUC/calibration but loses same-domain Ishu accuracy; source-slice diagnostics show the gains and losses are generator/category uneven, so keep it as an ablation rather than a headline method.
\end{itemize}
